data.title: Alexandria Q1 Preview: Expecting Positive Progress On Reset, 'Strong Buy' Long-Term
data.description:
Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) is undergoing a strategic reset, one that investors appear to have no interest in waiting out.

I believe sentiment will ultimately turn for the better over the long term, and I believe those that stick it out could be greatly rewarded with outsized market returns.

Shares in ARE have lost 75% of their value over the last five years. This includes a nearly 40% decline over the past year.

This coincides with the ongoing bear market in biotech, one of ARE’s largest sector exposures. Since 2021, availability of life science real estate has grown 7.5x. That’s proving to be a crushing supply-side statistic for ARE.

ARE is bearing the bear by doing what it has always done best. That is, maintaining its strong balance sheet and significantly adjusting capital needs.

For 2026, ARE intends to continue reducing the size of its existing asset base, while focusing on substantially growing its megacampuses.

Investors are skeptical and see ARE in a very much ‘show me’ mode. While sentiment among the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) community is bullish overall, much of the recent coverage has been at the ‘hold’ end of the spectrum.

For my part, I am strongly bullish, and I believe better days await ARE. This certainly is not going to happen overnight, and it's likely not going to happen following ARE’s Q1 release, which is set for the post-market hours of Monday, the 27th. But I ultimately believe ARE will overcome its challenges, and I can see its shares rebounding back to the $90/share level over the medium to long term.

ARE Stock Key Metrics

Stripping out the biotech noise and just looking at ARE as a REIT, I believe that ARE still looks like one of the highest-quality real estate platforms in the public markets.

The portfolio ended 2025 at 90.9% occupancy, up sequentially, with +1.2M square feet (“SF”) of leasing in Q4 alone. That’s not bad for a bear market. Not to mention that this was also the strongest quarter of the year.

In addition, nearly 900K SF of signed leases are set to commence in 2H26. This represents +$52M of incremental annual rent. Not small change by any means.

Furthermore, Megacampuses account for 78% of annual rental revenue and are outperforming market occupancy in ARE’s three largest markets by 19%. That’s a massive tell about where tenant demand still exists. It’s also validation, in my view, of ARE’s strategy to pivot to these megacampuses through 2026.

Lease duration remains healthy at 7.5 years, with ~3% annual rent steps on 97% of leases and 53% of rent from investment-grade (“IG”) or large-cap public tenants. Additionally, EBITDA margins came in at an impressive 70% for Q4.

From a balance sheet perspective, ARE has +$5.3B of liquidity, an industry-leading 12+ year average debt maturity, and leverage at 5.7x net debt/EBITDA. That’s a fortress, in my eyes.

At roughly $46/share and ~170M shares outstanding, ARE’s equity market cap sits near $7.8B. When viewed against its stabilized NOI base, the stock is effectively implying a double-digit cap rate on irreplaceable megacampus life science assets. In my view, that’s a valuation that simply does not line up with the quality of the portfolio.

What Is The Outlook For ARE Stock?

The near- to medium-term outlook for ARE definitely doesn’t look great.

Founder and Chairman, Joel Marcus, didn’t sugarcoat this, either. In his remarks and in ARE’s investor presentation, ARE mentioned that 2025 marked the fifth year of a life science bear market, with oversupply across key submarkets and regulatory uncertainty adding even more pressure to an already strained biotech funding environment.

That, in turn, has translated into lower occupancy, softer same-property NOI, and near-term leasing friction that will likely carry into early 2026.

What’s more important, however, is what’s in ARE’s immediate control, and with regards to that, I believe ARE has clearly mapped a response that will enable it to overcome its challenges over the medium to long term.

ARE completed +$1.5B of dispositions in Q4 alone and laid out plans for roughly +$2.9B more in 2026, with a heavy emphasis on land and noncore assets.

The goal is straightforward. Shrink the asset base, reduce future capital needs, concentrate around Megacampuses, and preserve one of the strongest balance sheets in the REIT universe.

At the same time, leasing momentum is quietly improving, vacant space is being addressed aggressively, and nearly 900K SF of signed leases are slated to begin contributing rent in the back half of 2026.

Granted, the near term will still look messy. But the actions being taken today by ARE should benefit the REIT over the longer term.

What To Watch When ARE Reports Q1 Results?

When ARE reports its Q1, investors should be prepared for numbers that may not look pretty on the surface.

ARE has already guided to a first-quarter occupancy dip tied to +1.2M SF of lease expirations with expected downtime. Roughly 60% of those rolled in mid-January, meaning Q1 will fully reflect the vacancy before backfilling begins.

On top of that, a 171 SF lease termination in South San Francisco creates additional temporary vacancy until the new tenant commences in the second half of 2026. There’s also an expected +$6M quarterly rent reduction tied to potential tenant wind-downs.

Furthermore, same-property NOI is expected to be weakest in the first half of the year before improving later in 2026 as signed leases begin to contribute and dispositions reduce drag from noncore assets.

Investors should also watch leverage, which ARE noted could temporarily rise by 1 to 1.5 turns in Q1 due to lower EBITDA before improving as asset sales close later in the year.

In my view, the key won’t be the headline numbers. It will be leasing progress, disposition updates, and signs that the reset plan is moving forward on schedule.

Is ARE Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I believe ARE is a ‘strong buy,’ but only for investors willing to think in years, not quarters.

In my view, the market is valuing ARE as if its real estate is permanently impaired. However, a double-digit implied cap rate on megacampus assets, a fortress balance sheet, and long-duration leases to IG tenants simply do not reconcile. I believe that disconnect is exactly where opportunity is born.

Of note is that while the Board refreshed a +$500M share repurchase authorization, ARE made it clear that no buybacks are assumed for 2026. Some would view that as a negative.

I, on the other hand, actually view that as a positive signal of discipline. Rather than using cash to prop up the share price, ARE is prioritizing dispositions, deleveraging, and reducing future capital needs. In my view, these are the right steps to strengthen the platform and unlock value the right way.

Yes, the stock could fall further after Q1. Sentiment is poor, and the numbers will likely reflect the near-term pain already outlined. But I view that as noise relative to what’s happening underneath. Asset sales, capital discipline, megacampus concentration, and signed leases that begin contributing meaningfully in 2026.

I ultimately believe this feels less like a typical REIT downturn and more like a generational dislocation in a best-in-class platform. If ARE executes on this reset, and history suggests it will, I believe today’s price could look like a rare long-term entry point.

data.title: Ternium: Retest Of $45+ Likely As We Approach Q1 Earnings Release (Rating Upgrade) (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

Our last commentary on Ternium S.A. (TX) was in September 2022, when we placed a 'Sell' rating on the steel manufacturer when shares were trading just below $30 a share. Shares did, in fact, drop from that point to close to the $27 mark in the following month, but have since rebounded aggressively to presently trade above $40 a share over three years later. We are covering Ternium again as we approach the release of the company's first-quarter earnings for FY26, due on the 5th of May next.

First, though, a few words on where Ternium resources are aligned in the steel industry and where, in particular, it demonstrates strength relative to the competition at this very moment. The Luxembourg-based outfit principally operates internationally in both the steel and mining sectors. For example, Mexico is a significant hub for the company, where the Pesqueria location is in an expansion phase. In Brazil and neighbouring Latin American countries, Ternium's flat steel positioning remains prominent, with multiple industries being serviced throughout the region. Astuteness concerning investments with respect to the company's strong production capacity, along with its integrated supply chain (facilitating synergies, leading to lower costs), has positioned Ternium well in the markets it operates in.

The company recently received an upgrade from Bank of America on expected rising US flat steel prices, but this came on the back of a downgrade by Scotiabank in March this year, citing the USMCA agreement and persistent US steel tariffs as roadblocks to sustained upside in the stock.

Suffice it to say, there are a lot of moving parts in Ternium, but investors need to remember that the Steel industry is cyclical and can change on a dime in what could be perceived to be an industry projected to grow aggressively going forward. What we are pointing out here is that Ternium is expected to grow its earnings by 3%+ and 30%+ (EPS of $4.75 - current indication for FY26), but recent FY27 revisions have been ugly, confirmed by a contraction of 23%+ over the past 30 days.

Therefore, being chartists and believing the market discounts everything, let's head over to the charts to see how Ternium currently sets up from a technical standpoint as we approach the release of the company's first quarter earnings.

Long-Term Technicals

When we go to the 25-year chart, we see that the stock recently took out its 2021 highs and currently looks like it is preparing a second attempt is in the offing. From a technical standpoint, the fact that Ternium's

  1. Long-term ADX trend-following is rising
  2. Long-term OBV volume-accumulation indicator is rising
  3. Recently delivered a golden cross (10-month moving average crossed above its 40-month counterpart)

gives plenty of optimism that prices could indeed keep rallying post the Q1 release. However, on the flip side, given that a divergence exists in the RSI momentum indicator, caution is still required at this juncture for the following reason.

Although many investors will be focused on the normalised earnings target of $0.90 and expected revenues of $3.96 billion in Q1, we will be more focused on the necessary conditions for Ternium to keep growing for many years to come. This is important given the above-stated bearish revisions and how the market overall will digest the company's numbers. Suffice it to say, if a breakout materialises on muted EBITDA and margin strength, this will be a direct message by the market that it has priced in (for now) the bearish revisions, all things remaining equal. Position sizing, therefore, should be adjusted accordingly if funds are to be put to work from that point.

Intermediate 5-Year Chart

When we look at the intermediate chart, we see a very strong trend from April of last year to February of this year, with the price topping $45 per share. Furthermore, there is no such divergence in the stock's RSI intermediate momentum indicator; in fact, quite the opposite. Furthermore, given that the share price remained above Ternium's 40-week moving average after the Q4 earnings release in February, this lends weight to a further retest of all-time highs, if not a possible enduring breakout after the company's upcoming Q1 earnings release.

Remember, trends on the intermediate chart ultimately transcend onto the long-term monthly charts over time. Although Ternium's ADX trend-following indicator ended up above a 50 reading on the intermediate chart, the recent softening below 40 definitely provides the ammunition for more gains in Ternium, all things remaining equal.

12-Month Technical Chart

More than anything on shorter-term charts, we look at volume trends to see if the present move is indeed sustainable. As we see below on the 12-month chart, although Ternium's OBV line continues to rise, buying volume on the whole since the February bottom is on the wane. This means that although buyer accumulation continues to be the order of the day in this play, participation may, in fact, be weakening, all things remaining equal. This is further reason for investors to wait until the release of the company's first-quarter earnings numbers before making a firm decision in TX.

Being chartists, we believe volume trends precede share-price action, all things remaining equal. Nevertheless, when we couple mixed volume signals with the fact that we may still be dealing with a possible false breakout in early February this year, caution is warranted at this juncture.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, although Ternium is projected to report strong growth in FY26 and FY27, investors should be wary of the cyclical nature of the steel industry, in that these sound projections may be beginning to be priced in. From a technical standpoint, many indicators are in alignment with a sustained up move, although near-term volume levels demonstrate reduced participation as we approach the company's Q1 release. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Tesla Earnings Just Reopened The Road To $415
data.description:
Following Tesla, Inc.’s (TSLA) disappointing Q1 delivery print released earlier this year (the second-worst since 2022), the company has reversed the narrative with better-than-expected earnings results for the quarter. The company grew total revenue by 16% y/y to almost $22.4 billion in Q1, outperforming the average consensus estimate of $22.2 billion. Meanwhile, non-GAAP EPS of $0.41 also beat the average market expectation of $0.35. Coupled with a modest capex outlay to kick off the year relative to Tesla’s raised full year investment plan, the company generated positive FCF of $1.4 billion in Q1 – far outpacing the average consensus estimate of a $1.9 billion outflow.

While Tesla’s moderating EV sales is evident of its increasingly structural pivot towards an AI- and autonomous-centric roadmap, the company’s latest earnings outperformance continues to support durable FCF accretion from its core business. This continues to represent critical support to the ongoing execution of its capital-intensive ambitions ahead – especially as Tesla eyes capex spend of $25 billion this year, which is higher than previous expectations benchmarked at $20 billion.

The stock’s pullback this year also meaningfully de-risks against ongoing pressure in auto sales, making room for renewed upside as visibility into its longer-term AI and autonomy roadmap improves. Specifically, the stock’s current price at about $388 per share approximates my previous price target, which accounts primarily for Tesla’s operations that’ve shown tangible fundamental visibility. However, its latest earnings update positions Tesla for further upside as visibility into longer-term Robotaxi and Optimus monetization improves.

In the following analysis, I provide an updated sum-of-the-parts (“SOTP”) analysis for each of Tesla’s existing auto, energy generation and storage (“EGS”) and full self-drive ("FSD") businesses, while also incorporating an incremental valuation uplift for its emerging Robotaxi and Optimus opportunities. Taken together, Tesla is expected to be well positioned for another leg up towards a price target of $415 per share based on its current roadmap and anticipated execution progress.

Automotive

Tesla’s automotive sales remain challenged by a tepid demand backdrop, blighted by intensifying competition and a global scale back of EV adoption subsidies. This continues to be a key overhang on Tesla’s existing fundamentals and near-term valuation outlook, as its core auto operations remain the company’s primary FCF driver. Case in point – the stock’s close to 10% decline in the first week of April highlights sensitivity to the company’s weak Q1 delivery print released earlier this month.

Yet Tesla’s latest Q1 earnings outperformance underscores a balanced act between preserving FCF while onboarding a structural pivot away from traditional auto sales towards AI and autonomy developments. Despite disappointing Q1 deliveries which only grew 6% y/y, auto sales expanded 20% y/y during the quarter. This was primarily driven by a 13% y/y increase in ASP to the $43,000 range – its highest in more than two years. While management did not provide specific details on drivers of the ASP expansion, it could be reflective of a favorable mix shift towards premium Model S/X sales based on the 25% y/y growth in other model deliveries in Q1. This has likely helped to partially offset headwinds from the continued wind-down of auto credit sales amid regulatory changes that went into effect last year.

Looking ahead, impending catalysts that support restored growth in 2026 auto deliveries include higher fuel prices – especially in Europe – given ongoing implications of intensifying global geopolitical tensions, the pullback in BEV investments across ICE incumbents, as well as an impending Model S/X inventory clear-out. This is corroborated by management’s disclosure that Tesla has achieved its strongest auto sales backlog in two years during Q1, underscoring that the sales slump may have bottomed.

Specifically, Europe has turned from one of Tesla’s best-performing markets to its slowest growing region in recent years due to intensifying competition, as well as adverse implications from Musk’s political engagements. Yet Tesla has reported renewed delivery growth in the region during Q1 “for the first time in over a year,” likely driven by a 150% sequential expansion in deliveries within France and Germany. Although Tesla continues to face elevated competition in Europe due to strong EV exports from its Chinese rivals, renewed momentum in the region is expected to remain durable as elevated fuel prices due to the Iran conflict could reaccelerate the transition to electric.

Meanwhile, legacy OEMs like Ford (F), Stellantis (STLA), and GM (GM) have also recently recorded impairments to their respective EV operations – totaling more than $53 billion – due to slowing demand. This suggests a structural shift in their focus back towards ICE and hybrid powertrains instead, which could potentially reduce direct BEV competition for Tesla.

The impending clearance of Model S/X inventory is also expected to boost near-term deliveries. Despite anticipated discounts, the premium segment’s higher MSRP is expected to represent an incremental growth and earnings tailwind for Tesla this year.

Taken together, I expect Tesla to grow auto deliveries by 10% y/y to 1.8 million units this year. The assumption reflects stronger reacceleration in Tesla’s “other models” sales compared to the Model 3/Y, which is in line with observations in Q1, as well as the impending sell-through of existing Model S/X inventory. The anticipated mix shift towards its more expensive line-up is expected to drive ASP expansion of 4% y/y in 2026, resulting in 12% y/y revenue growth in total automotive revenues towards $77.6 billion this year.

In valuing the automotive unit, I’ve applied a 1.3x NTM sales multiple on the segment’s projected 2026 revenue. The 1.3x NTM P/S multiple reflects the current average observed across both legacy automakers and EV pureplays – particularly in China – on a relative basis to Tesla’s anticipated growth. This results in an estimated price of $29 per share attributable to Tesla’s automotive operations.

Energy Generation and Storage

Tesla’s EGS unit delivered 8.8GWh of storage in Q1, representing a 15% y/y and 38% sequential decline despite the anticipated near-term tailwind from scaling power-intensive AI infrastructure deployments. The sizable drop continues to reflect inherent volatility in Tesla’s quarter-to-quarter energy storage deployments, despite the strong 2026 backlog.

Yet similar to its automotive operations, EGS’ earnings results have performed better than expected, with related sales having dropped only 12% y/y despite the 15% y/y reduction in energy storage deployments. More importantly, management expects total 2026 deployments to be higher than 2025 levels, underscoring reacceleration through the remainder of the year. This continues to underscore durable scalability as deployments ramp, offsetting management’s earlier caution of margin compression risks due to “increased low-cost competition” and tariff-driven uncertainties.

Taken together, ESG remains well-positioned fundamentally in capitalizing on structural tailwinds from AI-driven electricity demand growth. It also represents an increasingly mission critical component to the vertically integrated energy capabilities Tesla will need to execute its long-term AI and autonomy roadmap. As discussed in my previous coverage on Tesla, EGS’ Megapack technologies are positioned to directly address U.S. grid capacity supply constraints and inherent electrical swings that result from compute-intensive demands of AI workloads.

Specifically, the upcoming Megapack 3, which will begin productions outside of Houston later this year, is expected to incorporate Tesla Energy’s proprietary “autonomous, grid-forming controls” technology, which directly addresses grid stability constraints currently faced by compute-intensive AI data center deployments. The innovation is expected to increase EGS’ alignment with scaling AI infrastructure demands and reinforce visibility into its long-term storage deployment growth.

Taken together, Tesla’s EGS operations are expected to maintain strong double digit percentage growth into the longer-term, with my forecast estimating expansion at a 10-year CAGR of 25% between 2025 and 2035.

Based on the application of a 3.5x multiple on estimated 2035 revenue, Tesla’s EGS is expected to contribute an intrinsic value of $59 per share to the stock. The valuation analysis uses estimated 2035 revenues as a benchmark as that’s when Tesla’s EGS business is expected to generate a more stabilized earnings profile. Meanwhile, the 3.5x forward P/S multiple reflects the currently traded levels of comparable peers like LG Energy, which operates at a mature scale and cost structure. The implied equity value is then discounted back 9 years at an 8.4% WACC, in line with Tesla’s capital structure and risk profile, to reflect the present value of EGS.

FSD

Q1 represents the first full quarter of Tesla’s transition to a subscription-based model for FSD sales in certain regions. Recall that management had previously disclosed 1.1 million paid FSD customers globally, with upfront license purchases representing 70% of the mix. The number of paid FSD subscribers has expanded to 1.3 million in Q1, with monthly subscriptions being the key growth driver as Tesla continues to work on winding-down upfront license purchases. Assuming the 70% upfront license mix remains unchanged, it’d imply an FSD subscription annual recurring revenue (“ARR”) of $463.3 million at the current rate of $99 per month. This compares to about $392 million in the prior quarter, representing close to 20% sequential growth in FSD subscription sales during Q1.

While management had previously warned the transition to fully subscription-based FSD sales will levy a short-term impact on automotive margins, given the reduced mix of lump-sum license profits, Tesla’s latest results had outperformed expectations. Specifically, the company reported auto (ex-credit) gross margins of 19% in Q1, stabilizing from 18% in Q4 and 13% in Q1 2025. While management has partially attributed the improvement to one-time warranty true-downs and tariff reliefs, the results likely underscore sustained earnings accretion as FSD deployments scale – especially as performance improves.

Specifically, FSD is now rolling out version 14.3 and on track to deploying version 15 by early 2027 at the latest. The transition to version 15 will encompass a “complete overhaul of software architecture running on AI4,” which will likely improve visibility into unsupervised deployment over the longer-term. This is corroborated by recent approval for Tesla to deploy FSD in the Netherlands, reinforcing prospects of regulatory greenlighting for EU-wide deployment starting later Q2. More importantly, Tesla’s also disclosed receipt of FSD approval in China, though broader greenlighting for on-road deployment is still in the works. For now, management expects full FSD deployment approval in China by Q3, which could represent a key catalyst for both auto and FSD sales, as the region continues to represent Tesla’s fastest-growing and largest EV market.

Looking ahead, deeper overseas market penetration – particularly in China – as well as incremental adoption in the U.S. will remain kye catalysts for the stock. Yet this will likely remain a function of continued technological advancements. Admittedly, it’s likely near-term FSD revenue expansion will remain dependent on auto delivery sales growth. However, longer-term adoption, which is expected to be more structural, will remain contingent on FSD advancements towards Level 4+ autonomy. And Tesla continues to exhibit improving visibility on that front, as corroborated by management’s expectations for FSD unsupervised to reach customer cars by the end of the year, which aligns with the upcoming deployment timeline for version 15.

Specifically, achieving L4+ autonomy will be a structural inflection for FSD adoption and monetization in the long-run. It’s likely total FSD revenues at scale will consist of both monthly subscriptions to Tesla and non-Tesla vehicles. On Tesla FSD subscriptions, my forecast expects an 80% take-rate by 2035 when the company’s expected to have achieved L4+ autonomy. This implies a global Tesla FSD installed base of about 32 million, generating annual revenue of $16.1 billion by 2035. The estimate also assumes an eventual decline in the FSD subscription rate to about $52 per month as adoption scales, approximating levels observed across key utilities like broadband. Meanwhile, FSD uptake by non-Tesla connected vehicles is expected at about 10% – or 33 million subscriptions – by 2035. Based on a $60 monthly subscription rate for non-Tesla connected vehicles, which reflects a slight premium to the price charged on Tesla-brand vehicles, this implies revenue of $23.4 billion. Taken together, Tesla is expected to generate total revenue of $39.5 billion from FSD sales by 2035.

My valuation of Tesla’s FSD business applies a 10x forward sales multiple to 2035 revenue, discounted back 9 years at an 8.4% WACC. The 10x forward P/S multiple applied approximates the current software peer group average, while 2035 serves as the relevant benchmark year as it reflects at which FSD operations are expected to reach structural adoption and scale more meaningfully in the U.S. and overseas. This generates a $54 per share contribution from FSD to Tesla’s valuation.

Robotaxi

Tesla’s Robotaxi rollout remains a key gauge of tangibility to the stock’s current autonomy premium. Although management continues to refrain from bifurcating Tesla’s Robotaxi revenue, having previously noted that the “metrics are not meaningful” in the early phase of deployment, the company continues to make progress on the broader U.S. rollout. Specifically, Tesla has now added Dallas and Houston to its existing footprint, with expectations for the broader Robotaxi fleet to expand sequentially through 2026 with penetration into incremental regions. This will be complemented by expectations for deployment of unsupervised Robotaxis across 12 states by the end of the year, with management expecting the segment’s revenue to become significant starting 2027.

Admittedly, global rollout is likely still years away given elevated regulatory hurdles that’ve continued to gate-keep FSD expansion overseas. However, consistent progress on the U.S. rollout continues to improve visibility into Robotaxi’s monetization outlook, potentially re-catalyzing an autonomy-driven rally for the stock in the near-term.

Specifically, my forecast expects Tesla’s Robotaxi operations to contribute $132 per share to the stock’s total value. Similar to the valuation analysis for FSD, the 10x forward sales multiple applied in valuing Robotaxi is benchmarked to the current software peer group average. Its relevance is supported by Tesla’s asset-lite Robotaxi strategy, which primarily leverages a software-driven model that underpins structurally higher margins as adopting scales. Meanwhile, estimated 2040 Robotaxi revenue serves as a fundamental benchmark for the valuation analysis, as that’s when the business is expected to approach mature scale, making the application of today’s industry average multiple more appropriate. The resulting equity value estimate is discounted back 24 years at an 8.4% WACC.

Optimus

The “We, Robot” launch event in 2024, alongside the recent inclusion of Optimus sales targets into Musk’s newly approved compensation plan, have effectively increased the durability of the AI and robotics premium currently embedded in the Tesla stock.

Recall that management has previously cited three primary challenges to the development of humanoid robots – namely, achieving human-like hand dexterity, integrating high-performance AI, and scaling production. And Tesla’s continued to progress with improved visibility into Optimus’ deployment outlook. Specifically, Tesla indicated that Optimus 3 is “almost ready to demonstrate,” with expectations for an official launch in mid-2027. This effectively reinforces on-track progress towards anticipated Optimus 3 start of production later this year at Fremont, with management targeting viable external use cases outside of Tesla by the end of next year.

But increasing our internal production for testing and then probably being able to have Optimus be useful outside of Tesla sometime next year.

Source: Tesla Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

Looking ahead, industry predicts a global fleet of 930 million cumulative humanoid robots by 2050 when deployments approach mature scale, with China accounting for about 300 million units. Assuming a 25% market share for Optimus, which is supported by management’s view that Tesla’s humanoid robot technology “will be much more capable” than intensifying competition from developments in China, this would imply an Optimus installed base of about 233 million units. From anticipated mass market adoption starting in 2030 through 2050, the 20-year timeline reflects an average annual unit sales of about 11.6 million. I’ve used this as the basis for estimating 2050 Optimus revenue, which is likely conservative as annual deployments are expected to accelerate over time. Based on Musk’s previously cited price target for Optimus at as much as $30,000 per unit, the business is expected to generate close to $350 billion in annual revenue by 2050.

I’ve applied a 10x forward sales multiple on projected 2050 Optimus revenue. Discounted back 24 years at an 8.4% WACC, the segment is expected to contribute $141 per share towards Tesla’s valuation.

Price Considerations

Taken together, details from Tesla’s latest earnings update support momentum towards a SOTP valuation of $415 per share.

Consistent with the foregoing analysis, key near-term catalysts include continued execution consistency in FSD advancements and Robotaxi rollout, as well as the impending Megapack 3 and Optimus 3 rollout.

Risks to Consider

In addition to macroeconomic and regulatory dynamics that remain a wildcard on near-term fundamentals across Tesla’s existing operations, investor sentiment also faces potential disruption from the upcoming SpaceX (SPACE) IPO. Specifically, Musk’s rocket venture is targeting a valuation of close to $2 trillion at its upcoming IPO, which would make it the most expensive public market debut in history. The sizable listing could risk a capital shift towards SpaceX at the expense of Tesla, and hamper the latter’s recovery.

Meanwhile, Tesla’s increasing capex intensity is poised to invite incremental debt funding. Specifically, the company is raising its full year capex guidance from $20 billion previously to at least $25 billion. This could potentially dampen near-term confidence in Tesla’s FCF accretion outlook, with the impending increase in capital costs potentially weighing further on the ROI visibility needed for the stock’s recovery.

Final Thoughts

Taken together, Tesla’s latest earnings outperformance has further reinforced confidence in the company’s strategic pivot towards AI and autonomy, underscoring durability to incremental upside from emerging FSD, Robotaxi and Optimus monetization. Although near-term pressure on EV demand remains a relevant overhang on the stock’s recovery outlook, especially given Tesla’s intensifying capex spend trajectory, the Q1 results demonstrate capability in balancing earnings accretion against an increasingly capital-intensive roadmap. This is expected to reinforce visibility into its long-term ROI trajectory, and support the stock’s next leg higher towards the estimated SOTP valuation of $415 per share as execution consistency continues to materialize across Tesla’s autonomy, AI and robotics developments.

data.title: Why I've Stopped Buying REITs
data.description:
My investment objectives are purely tied to steady income growth at such a rate that would by a certain date allow me to live off dividends without touching the principal.

Obviously, I want to reach the promised land as soon as possible with minimal investments and stress.

One of the main elements that enables this to happen is putting capital to work in durable income-producing securities, which offer tangible yields. This way I immediately get the most bang for my buck, enriching my monthly contributions to buy new income-producing assets at a larger scale (i.e., building up my income machine faster).

In this context, REITs, theoretically, come into play quite well as they:

  • Provide decent yields.
  • Generate inflation-protected cash flows.
  • Offer the necessary resiliency and durability.

Of course, not all REITs or REIT sectors are created equal, but with some investigation and cherry-picking, the aforementioned items can be achieved.

However, despite these positives and the fact that I've personally deployed a notable part of my portfolio in high-quality equity REITs, I have to say that almost the entire asset class has become unattractive.

I haven't bought REITs for almost a year (this includes REIT dividend allocations to other assets).

Let me explain why.

REIT Yields Are Too Low

Most REITs with somewhat reasonable risk levels are simply yielding too low to move the needle from the current income perspective. This is especially the case if we adjust the yields with inflation.

A good way to illustrate the issue is to put REIT yields side by side with those that are offered from other typical income-oriented investor asset classes.

So, in the table below, I have compared REIT yields, 3-year dividend growth, and 3-year total return performance (in the form of a diversified benchmark, the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ)) to 7 other asset classes, which are commonly explored by durable income investors.

Asset class/factors Yield 3-year CAGR 3-year TR
Fixed-income CEFs (PDI) 15.2% 0.0% 53.2%
BDCs (BIZD) 13.0% 0.9% 29.5%
Covered call ETFs (JEPI) 8.3% -8.5% 33.1%
Midstream (AMLP) 7.7% 9.9% 68.7%
Quality CLOs (JAAA) 5.6% 10.6% 21.6%
Infrastructure (BIP) 4.7% 4.1% 21.0%
REITs (VNQ) 3.6% 0.6% 32.9%
Quality dividend growth (SCHD) 3.4% 6.3% 42.0%

The picture is clear. REITs offer one of the lowest dividend yields that come with completely unsatisfactory growth profiles.

On a total return basis, the performance has been somewhat acceptable, but price appreciation is largely irrelevant for me as someone who seeks to build up a portfolio to live off dividends.

Granted, there is a huge difference between BDC and REIT risks. Hence, it is only logical that REITs yield less.

However, I firmly believe that by leaning into a bit lower-yielding BDCs, which are underpinned by resilient fundamentals, we can still access sustainable double-digit current income streams. The same could be said about leveraged fixed income CEFs and covered call assets (i.e., clear possibilities to lock in juicy and durable dividends).

Granted, it is also possible to do the same thing in the REIT space. And this is what I have done by going long Realty Income (O) and EPR Properties (EPR), which yield 5.0% and 6.5%, respectively.

Yet three things here:

  • While the yields are higher, they still don't come close to my sweet spot range of 7% to 9% (which is where I currently see the best risk-adjusted durable income opportunities).
  • On an inflation-adjusted basis, the dividends look less enticing.
  • Income growth is simply not there, which would be expected given the relatively low yields.

For instance, in the past 5-year period, O has grown its dividend by ~3.5% per year, which is even lower than the realized inflation.

So, exactly this combination of relatively unattractive yields on an absolute level and suboptimal prospects of current income growth on an inflation-adjusted basis is what is holding me back from REITs.

The Future Doesn't Look Much Better

I see two potential paths for how REITs could get out of this situation. The first one seems very unlikely, and the second one is what I try to avoid in my portfolio.

I will quickly unpack each of them.

The first path would entail delivering much stronger dividend growth than so far. But, to be honest, I don't think that it is going to happen.

There are many reasons, but the main ones are:

  • The majority of REITs have approaching debt maturities, which yield less than what could be obtained in the debt markets now (i.e., unless rates go down significantly, many REITs will have to face headwinds to their FFO generation from unfavorable debt rollovers).
  • The highest-yielding REITs, which is where presumably most income investors would try to deploy capital, typically carry net lease structures. These net lease REITs tend to have very long lease maturity profiles (close to 10 years), which limit their ability to raise prices in a meaningful fashion.
  • Almost all REITs, which have long-duration lease contracts, have embedded annual rent escalators, which definitely help drive organic growth. However, in practice, the escalators are capped by 2%–3% levels, which in the context of today's inflation is not enough.

Here it might also be worth posing a rhetorical question of why, considering REIT inflation protection dynamics, they haven't delivered superior dividend growth in the recent years when the inflation was pretty high?

The answer lies in the combination of the aforementioned three points.

Finally, the thing that weakens this path (or potential motivation to buy REITs) even further is the availability of infrastructure and high-quality dividend growth vehicles, which provide a) similar if not higher yields than REITs and b) much stronger income growth prospects.

As reflected in the table above, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP) (infrastructure proxy) and the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) (quality dividend growth proxy) are good examples of that.

The second path would be associated with expanding REIT valuations, which would result in sizeable gains that investors could realize and deploy into other income-producing assets.

This is how REITs have performed since the moment when the Fed started to raise rates back in 2022:

During these years, most REITs have managed to secure FFO per share growth. So, the valuation multiples are even lower than what we could imply from the realized price correction. For instance, O's P/FFO is currently down by ~33% compared to the January 2022 level.

So, for REITs, which are high-duration assets, to enjoy a nice rally, the long-term yield has to come down.

In my humble opinion, this is a rather unlikely scenario.

Just think of it.

  • The war in Iran has already formed a different consensus on 2026 base rate expectations (from multiple cuts to an unchanged level).
  • As the most recent base rate cuts show, the long-term yield can remain unchanged or even go up a bit despite lower short-term interest rates.
  • The increased geopolitical tensions, rising fiscal deficits, massive government debt, etc., are all accommodative for a higher-for-longer long-term yield environment.

In other words, I wouldn't be so optimistic about the long-term yields going down any time soon.

However, even if I were wrong and the long-term yields dropped, which would expand REIT valuations, this is still not a scenario that I would enjoy.

In such a case, the reinvestment yields would compress, making REIT income compounding less attractive. On top of that, it would require selling assets to shift portfolio exposures towards higher income-producing assets. This is what I want to avoid as a long-term, buy-and-forget income investor.

The Bottom Line

Don't get me wrong.

There is definitely a merit in holding select high-quality REITs in an income-focused portfolio. If not from a yield-enhancement perspective, then definitely from the diversification perspective.

Currently, I have a bit less than 20% of my portfolio placed in REITs.

Having said that, the current setup in the REIT space is unfavorable for accelerated high-quality income compounding.

The opportunity cost that is associated with REIT investments is too high given the presence of more attractive durable income-producing alternatives (as outlined in the table above).

My current plan is to organically (by investing new and REIT dividend capital in other asset classes) reduce my REIT exposure to 12%–15%. After a 20% REIT system-wide sell-off, I would take another look at this asset class.

data.title: Under Armour: Likely A Value Trap With A Turnaround That Needs Execution
data.description:
Introduction

Under Armour (UAA) has been on my watchlist for some time, and with the company reporting its Q4'26 results in three weeks, I wanted to provide my thoughts on what to expect for FY'27. The short version is that this is a turnaround story that still has not turned. Revenue continues to shrink, margins remain under pressure, and the brand is still fighting an identity crisis that has been years in the making.

In this article, I'll walk through the puts and takes from the previous quarter, where I see the core issues with the business, and why I remain on the sidelines even at a seemingly depressed valuation.

A tough quarter that confirms the turnaround has a long way to go

Over the last few quarters, Under Armour's results have been pretty underwhelming, and Q3’26 was no exception. Revenues declined 5% year over year to $1.31 billion, marginally ahead of the company's own outlook, and management acknowledged that roughly 100bps of that came from a timing pull-forward of wholesale deliveries out of Q4.

When we look at what led to the results, North America has been weak, falling 10% compared to last year on continued wholesale weakness, and APAC has declined 5%. EMEA was the one bright spot, growing 6%, and Latin America posted a strong 20% gain. By product, footwear continued to be a disaster (down 12%) with declines across most categories. Apparel fell 3% and accessories dropped 3%, as shown below.

Under Armour’s profitability told a similar story of weakness. Gross margin came in at 44.4%, down 310bps year over year and in line with guidance. The biggest drivers were 200bps of pressure from U.S. tariffs and 140 bps from pricing headwinds in a promotional North American environment.

Further down the income statement, operating income was negative, but even on an adjusted basis, it came in at $26 million, which is paltry relative to the company’s $1.3 billion of revenue (a margin of just 2%). As I’ll discuss shortly, it’s a sobering reminder of just how badly the business needs a turnaround.

The saving grace for Under Armour, in my view, is that it doesn’t have a balance sheet problem. Inventory was down 2% year over year to just over $1 billion, which is an encouraging sign that the company's efforts to clean up the assortment are bearing fruit. Under Armour ended the quarter with cash of $465 million and total debt of $990 million, implying net debt of $525 million, with no borrowings outstanding under the $1.1 billion revolving credit facility and $600 million in restricted investments fully set aside to cover all remaining principal and interest on senior notes due in June. On capital allocation, UAA did not make any share repurchases but did pay down about $200 million in revolver borrowings during the quarter.

Looking at the company’s credit profile, Under Armour now has only two bond maturities. The nearest one is the 3.25% June 15 2026 bond, which is a $600 million issue that trades at 99.78 cents on the dollar. Given the cash on the balance sheet, a lot of that is likely earmarked to take this issue out completely at maturity. The longer dated bond, the 7.25% July 2030s, is a $400 million issue that currently trades for 103.20 cents on the dollar for a spread of 254bps and a yield of 6.14% (source: Bloomberg). With a credit rating of BB- from S&P, the company is still considered high yield but has made good progress so far this year, as shown below.

Where the problems are at Under Armour

It’s clear that the decline in sales and the margin contraction are symptoms of bigger problems at the company. The honest answer is that Under Armour's problems didn't start recently, and in fact they've been building for nearly a decade. Historically, the company has spent most of that time either misdiagnosing them or changing leadership before anyone had a real chance to fix them. At its peak around 2015, Under Armour was competing well with Nike (NKE), posting years of strong revenue growth and carrying a market cap north of $20 billion. Today, the stock trades at a fraction of those levels. Revenue has been declining for multiple years running, and the brand finds itself caught in an identity crisis that it still hasn't fully resolved.

The core problem, in my view, is that Under Armour never successfully made the transition from a niche performance brand to a brand with broader cultural appeal. It was built on the back of compression gear and on-field credibility, which is a great foundation, but the world moved on. The athleisure trend changed what consumers wanted from sportswear, and brands like Lululemon (LULU) and newer entrants like On Running and Hoka captured that shift while Under Armour was slow to respond. Lululemon went from a yoga niche to a $27 billion force. On and Hoka built cult followings in running with double-digit growth year after year. Meanwhile, Under Armour tried to be all things to all people and was expanding into too many categories, too many SKUs, with too many price points. That ended up being the clear choice for essentially nobody outside of its core high school football base.

The brand also leaned too heavily on discounting and promotions for too long, which is one of the most damaging things a consumer brand can do. When you train shoppers to wait for a sale, you basically erode pricing power and brand perception simultaneously. In Q4’25, the company’s e-commerce revenue fell 27% as they intentionally reduced promotional activity.

Finally, there’s also been turnover with senior leadership personnel at the company. The former CEO Kevin Plank stepped down in early 2020 as the company had been dealing with SEC investigations and reports of a toxic workplace culture. His successor, Patrik Frisk, lasted just two years before being pushed out. Colin Browne served as interim CEO. Then Stephanie Linnartz, a well-regarded Marriott executive, was brought in with considerable fanfare in early 2023 and given a three-year turnaround mandate. She lasted 13 months. All that is to say that Under Armour burned through four CEOs in four years. That's certainly not what you want to see for any business.

Issues within the footwear business

Footwear has been a persistent failure that management has consistently underestimated. The mistake, as management admitted on the latest call, was chasing volume through assortment expansion without building the scalable franchise demand that actually supports a healthy footwear business.

For multiple seasons, we tried to grow by expanding the assortment, more styles, more price points, more incremental updates without consistent demand or the scale to support it. That diluted volume pressured margins and increased inventory risk.

The result was a bloated line that diluted margins, confused consumers, and gave wholesale partners little reason to prioritize the brand. Year-to-date footwear sales are down 14% even as the company claims to be mid-turnaround. Nike and Adidas (ADDYY) built their footwear businesses around iconic franchises with clear identities. But Under Armour still hasn't cracked that code.

To address this, management essentially boils down to turnaround to one core idea: sell far fewer things but sell them better and at higher prices. On the product side, CEO Kevin Plank was explicit about cutting SKUs aggressively. So far, the company already eliminated 25% of its SKU count in FY’25 and is going further with a new focus on consolidating raw materials, reducing fabric variety, and building cleaner good/better/best segmentation across every category. In footwear specifically, the company is exiting ‘low-productivity styles’, concentrating investment behind fewer franchises in training, running, and sportswear, and pushing average selling prices higher. Their goal is to get the footwear business back above $100 ASP consistently.

Why I’m skeptical for next quarter and 2026

Ultimately, I have my doubts about Under Armour since the company has been telling a version of this same story for several years and the numbers have continued to disappoint. The "fewer, better products at higher prices" narrative isn't new. If you go back to some of the old earnings call transcripts from 2023 from Linnartz's mandate, she too had the goal of reducing SKUs by 25% in 18 months. And even in Q4’24 when Plank became CEO, the narrative around premiumization was back.

What’s happened since then? The North American business is still down double digits, footwear has declined 14% year to date, and operating margins sit at 2%. There's also a structural problem that doesn't get enough attention, and that’s the fact that Under Armour spent years training consumers to buy its products on discount. Rebuilding pricing power from that starting point is an extraordinarily difficult and slow process. To make things worse, a full year of tariff headwinds hits in FY’27 versus only a partial year in FY’26, meaning the margin math actually gets harder before it gets easier even if execution improves.

Looking ahead to Q4, the most important number will be North America revenue. Under Armour’s CEO was explicit that December marked the bottom of the reset, so if North America is still declining double digits, that claim starts to look premature. Gross margin will also matter given the persistent tariff pressure, and management's ability to offset that through pricing and mix will be telling.

As for what else to watch for next quarter, since FY'26 will be wrapped with Q4, I'll be watching for commentary around how management views Under Armour long term and how they expect to turn it around. Even with specific actions that they've discussed, we've yet to see meaningful results show up on the P&L. In the last three months, 17 out of the 19 sell-side analysts who covered the stock have since revised their EPS expectations negatively, with revenue expected to fall 12.0% quarter over quarter.

Despite the issues, there are still a few positives

To be fair, there are some things going right. The balance sheet is in better shape than it was two years ago, inventory is coming down, EMEA continues to perform well, and HeatGear and ColdGear are doing alright. While the "sell more of less" philosophy might work, I think the company might have trouble raising their price points. As is often the case in turnarounds, you often need to provide customers with disproportionate value before you can start raising prices again.

One development worth mentioning is the involvement of Fairfax Financial Holdings (FFH:CA), the Canadian insurer and investment conglomerate. Over late 2025 and into early 2026, Fairfax aggressively accumulated Under Armour shares at depressed prices, spending roughly $71 million in late December before continuing to buy in January (source: Bloomberg). In January, they disclosed a roughly 22% stake across both share classes. My bet is that they will likely push the company towards a sale given the depressed valuation. But whether it pays off depends entirely on whether Under Armour can execute, and that to me remains far from certain.

Valuation isn't compelling

On TTM numbers, Under Armour trades at 11.1x EV/EBITDA, right in line with other apparel and footwear companies. While it’s important to recognize that EBITDA is likely depressed and could rebound if the company is turned around successfully, I don’t think investors are getting enough compensation to initiate a position here today.

As well, as evidenced by the sell-side estimates below, expectations already bake in a return to the $500+ million EBITDA level by the end of the decade. That seems optimistic but requires execution.

Bottom line

For the reasons I've laid out, I think Under Armour is a value trap, which is why I'm issuing a 'sell' rating. The stock looks cheap on the surface, the balance sheet has improved, and management is saying the right things. But turnarounds require execution, and various management teams that have come and gone have been delivering the same narrative for several years while the numbers continue to move in the wrong direction. The involvement of Fairfax Financial adds an interesting wrinkle and could ultimately be the catalyst that forces a strategic outcome. So it'll be interesting to see if they continue to buy shares or push for a sale. For now, though, I see no reason to get involved. Until North America stabilizes and footwear shows improvements, I would rather stay away and wait for more evidence that this ship is actually turning.

data.title: Orange: A High-Yield European Telecom Giant Still Trading At A Discount
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Orange S.A. (ORANY), I highlighted the accretive effects of the MásMóvil merger in Spain, rating them a Buy thanks to the stock still being undervalued despite being up 55% YTD at the time.

Despite being up another ~36.5% since the previous article was published, Orange is reiterated as a Buy, offering an attractive dividend, strong fundamentals and solid potential to continue growing thanks to their ambitious plans.

Long-Term Plans Advancing Well

Orange reported a good Q1 report, with a 6.6% increase in EBITDAaL and 3.5% growth in revenue thanks to double-digit growth in Africa and the Middle East (12.7%) and expectable performance in Europe (2.2%), while wholesale revenue grew by 6.1% thanks to fiber co-financing received in France.

Meanwhile, the accumulated MasOrange synergies are now expected to reach over €430 million, showing how there’s still significant potential even in Europe’s well-established telecom market alongside their high-growth regions.

As part of their long-term plan, Orange continues advancing on positioning itself as a trusted AI provider (expecting +€600 million in additional value generated from AI in 2028 vs. >€300 million in 2025), building their next-generations networks, resilience and efficiency and solidifying its presence through the previously announced MasOrange acquisition and similar moves.

This position is further reinforced by the recently announced being in exclusive negotiations (together with Bouygues Telecom and the Free–iliad Group) with Altice France Group for the acquisition of SFR, adding potential to see even more synergies, reflecting a total EV of €20.35 billion, with Orange taking a 27% stake under the proposal.

In line with the company’s strategic long-term plan, we saw Nokia (NOK) and Orange partner on AI-RAN using Nokia’s anyRAN 5G software and Nvidia (NVDA) AI infrastructure, exploring the possibilities and preparing for 6G, which comes a few months after Nvidia bought a stake in Nokia.

Regarding the guidance for 2026, Orange actually increased their EBITDAaL guidance and confirmed the other metrics, although these don’t include the re-consolidation of the new MasOrange stake (getting to a 100% stake, recently getting an approval from the European Commission), expecting organic cash flow of about €4 billion (compared to €3.65 billion in FY25) and targeting a reduction in net debt to around 2x in the medium-term.

Meanwhile, Orange has recently returned to the U.S. bond market through the issuance of $6 billion worth of bonds that was apparently heavily oversubscribed with maturities in 2029, 2031, 2033, 2036 and 2056, with initial coupons of 4%, 4.25%, 4.75%, 5% and 5.75% - related to the acquisition of the remaining 50% of MasOrange, which I talked about before, which comes after they issued €5 billion in November 2025 for yields ranging from 2.5% to 4.125% for the initial 50% acquisition and other purposes.

As for the dividend, Orange’s Board is going to propose €0.75 per share for 2026 and has set a floor of €0.79 per share for 2027, which translates into a yield of 4.18% this year and at least 4.40% based on today’s prices converted in EUR.

Macro Pressure Can Disappoint

Right now, the ongoing Iran conflict will be key for the company’s near-term performance, as it can affect Orange’s key markets for much longer than the military side of the conflict itself, while the latest developments show a re-escalation, with new threats despite peace talks reportedly advancing.

This is something that goes well beyond just oil and gas, which can also affect Orange’s margins and the financial stability of their consumers and partners, which is a particular issue for European telecom companies compared to American ones, as they can’t just pass these costs to the consumer as easily due to strict regulatory oversight and much harsher fines for the industry.

The ECB showed its willingness to hike rates very early into the conflict, which is a natural response to inflation, being one of the central bank’s main tools meant to combat it. This ultimately resulted in European borrowing costs jumping to 15-year highs recently, which can “counter” the company’s defensiveness.

Especially since we’re talking about a company like this, rate hikes or even a higher-for-longer interest environment can certainly be terrible, as it can result in significant jumps in interest expenses alongside a general slowdown, while their CAPEX and constant need for investments can also pile up. Although they have the advantage of flexibility in terms of tapping the European or American markets (as seen recently), that doesn’t mean they can’t be affected by higher borrowing costs across the board, which can slow down the company’s ambitions and potentially result in missing the market’s expectations.

Over the longer term however, I believe Orange can also benefit from a boost coming from a potential retreat from the dollar, as interest rates can fall and money would move away from U.S. treasuries, which can boost the attractiveness of strong foreign businesses like this that’s undergoing significant M&A and adjusting their portfolio.

Meanwhile, if we see the market’s attention move back to the fundamental issues that threatened the dollar (rate cuts, Japan’s potential U.S. treasury selloff, political/economic uncertainty, consumer weakness, etc.) before the Iran conflict, that can result in more demand for Orange and similar equities as well (even from Japan), as one of the largest companies in its industry and, in fact, one of the largest companies in Europe, paying a solid dividend and offering some levels of defensiveness alongside it.

Although Orange wasn’t involved in this one, I’ll also mention the rumors of a potential deal between Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY) and T-Mobile US (TMUS) - with the former already owning 53% of TMUS - which can be an interesting development that I considered when investing in U.S. telecom peers, who can use their solid cash flow to develop internationally, and having two strong pillars (in this case, the U.S. and Europe) can fuel significant growth in higher-potential regions, so I wouldn’t rule out the possibility of seeing Orange being involved in something like this as well eventually.

Risks Reiterated

As defensive as the company’s foundation may be, it’s important to remember that the stock is still not immune to a slowdown or recession, which becomes more and more likely with every extra day in which the Strait of Hormuz stays closed.

Also, even though there is potential to expand in Africa and the Middle East, that comes at a major cost, while mistakes in regard to their ongoing deals or even data privacy risks in Europe can be extremely costly compared to their American peers, as highlighted during previous coverage.

Valuation

For Orange’s DCF to Equity, based on their improved guidance, I’ll estimate a FCF of €4.1 billion in 2026, followed by €4.6 billion in 2027 and €5.2 billion in 2028 given their long-term target, possible thanks to their significant investing activity, synergies, and continued growth in key regions.

I’ll assume they can grow at a 4% CAGR until 2035 thanks to their potential to continue this momentum, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a conservative 11% discount rate to demand a solid margin of safety given the internal and macro risks, with 2.66 billion shares outstanding as of the Q1 2026 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of €60.97 billion, for an intrinsic value of €22.92 per share, meaning about $26.78 based on today’s conversion rate, still above the current $20.45 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

Orange is reiterated as a Buy, backed by a strong foundation and significant improvements thanks to their strategic plans, trading at an attractive valuation that implies a significant margin of safety.

There are risks to keep in mind given the industry and their bold moves, but the risk-reward is still attractive even after the stock went up since last covering them.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Danaher Corporation: Masimo Deal Adds Long-Term Potential But Near-Term Pressure
data.description:
Investment Thesis

After my first article about Danaher Corporation (DHR), the shares rose to the target price of $210 due to a steady recovery in revenue and ahead of the quarterly EPS forecast. Expecting this trend to continue, I maintained the Buy rating in the second article; however, after the news of the purchase of Masimo Corporation and the release of the Q4 report, the shares fell sharply in price. Given the new introductory and weak revenue data for Q1, which also failed to meet expectations, I am temporarily downgrading the company's rating from Buy to Hold. The company continues to generate a high FCF and remains one of the best in terms of business profitability; however, a strong increase in the debt burden will put pressure on net profit, and unforeseen transaction-related costs may negatively affect the overall marginality of the business. In addition, for the second quarter in a row, revenue has actually stagnated.

Masimo Deal: Long-Term Growth, Short-Term Pressure

Before proceeding to the analysis of the latest Q1 financial statements, I would like to analyze the situation with the purchase of Masimo Corporation, which was announced in early February. The essence of the deal is as follows: Danaher buys a company from MedTech, which develops devices that monitor the client's condition in real time. With this purchase, the company is strengthening the Diagnostics segment. Danaher management expects to receive synergies from the deal in the form of cost savings of $125 million and additional revenue of $50 million per year in a few years. Management also expects the deal to increase EPS by $0.15-$0.20 in the first year. The deal is currently under regulatory approval. Danaher aims to close the deal completely in the second half of 2026.

Now about the terms of the purchase: the deal is valued at $9.9 billion, or $180 per share, at a premium to the market price of 38%. The purchase will be financed partly through cash, partly through new debt. The exact proportions are not known, but given that the company had $5.7 billion in its accounts at the end of Q1, I would venture to assume that Danaher will spend $3-4 billion of its own money, and the remaining $6-7 billion will be raised through debt. Since the total debt at the end of the quarter was $18.48 billion, the new deal will lead to an increase in debt to $24-25 billion and net debt to $22-23 billion. Thus, TTM's Debt/EBITDA will grow to 3.15-3.20x, and TTM's Net Debt/EBITDA to 2.9-3.0x. In this case, the company is likely to focus on debt reduction and reduce share buybacks from the market.

What are the pros and cons of the deal? In fact, the advantages for Danaher are rather long-term, and the disadvantages are short-term. Over the next few years, the deal will help the company increase revenue growth, support operating profit, and overall profitability of the business. But this is provided that the company's integration is successful and the synergy really meets the expectations of management. There seem to be more disadvantages so far. Firstly, a 38% premium seems high for a company that demonstrates single digit revenue growth. In my opinion, Danaher is overpaying. Secondly, integration risks are emerging - it is far from certain that the final return on the transaction will meet management expectations. Thirdly, the debt burden will grow significantly, and interest costs will rise significantly. This creates pressure on net profit and FCF for shareholders, as management will now try to pay off the debt first. Of course, the debt situation will remain under control, as Danaher generates a high FCF of $5-6 billion per year, but now part of these funds will be used to repay the debt, or it could be used to pay shareholders through buybacks and increased dividends. Upon the release of the news about the deal, the shares fell sharply in price, which is logical - investors were wary of such a large transaction.

Stable Cash Flow and Margins Despite Weak Revenue Trends

The latest Q1 report turned out to be rather mixed - EPS managed to beat market expectations, but revenue stagnation continues. Revenue growth was only 3.5% yoy to $5,951 billion, of which only 0.5% resulted in business growth, and the remaining 3% is simply the effect of currency revaluation. In fact, sales have not increased, and demand remains weak. The Biotechnology segment made the main contribution, with an 11% increase in yoy to $1.797 billion. But even here, 4% is a currency revaluation. Revenue in Life Sciences increased by 3.5% yoy, of which business growth was only 0.5%, while the Diagnostics segment generally showed a 1.5% decrease in revenue to $2.417 billion, with an organic decrease of 4.0%. Thus, high demand remained only in the Biotechnology segment, and more specifically, for consumables from large pharmaceutical companies, while sales of equipment remained weak. Thus, although revenue increased on paper, there was no organic business growth. The Biotechnology segment simply compensated for the stagnation in Life Sciences and the decline in Diagnostics with its growth. The only good news is that the share of recurring revenue in total revenue increased to 84.5% from 83.9%.

Things are better at the level of operating profit. GAAP growth was 5.5% yoy, and non-GAAP growth was 5.6% yoy. As a result, the operating margin increased to 22.6% from 22.2%, and adjusted margin increased to 30.2% from 29.6%. The increase in the operational efficiency of the business was due to an increase in sales of more marginal products in total revenue. In addition, the share of SG&A and R&D decreased in the quarter from 32.4% to 31.3% and from 6.6% to 6.5%. Effective cost control and a more favorable product mix supported operating profitability in the absence of actual organic business growth. Danaher remains one of the most profitable companies in the sector.

The disappointment in the low revenue growth rates was partially offset by the outperformance of the actual EPS result relative to market expectations. This was facilitated by a 7.8% yoy increase in GAAP net income to 1.029 billion. As a result, GAAP EPS increased from $1.32 to $1.45, and adjusted EPS increased from $1.88 to $2.06. For comparison, consensus was at $1.94, which is 6.45% lower than the data from the financial statements. The net margin increased to 17.3% from 16.6%. The increase in net profit was driven by both strong operating results, lower interest expenses, which decreased by 12.5%, and higher interest income, which increased from $6 million to $27 million. With the successful completion of the upcoming transaction, interest costs will, of course, increase significantly, which will affect net profit.

Nevertheless, net profit is more of an accounting indicator, which includes a lot of non-monetary expenses and income. As investors, it is more important for us what happens to cash flows, since it is from them that the future large debt will be extinguished after the transaction, and it is from them that dividends are paid and shares are bought back from the market. OCF did not grow much, only by 1.77% yoy to $1.322 billion, while FCF grew by 4.7% yoy to $1.1 billion. Although the growth is not strong, FCF Yield still remains one of the highest among peers, reaching 18-20%.

Conservative Guidance Reflects Uncertain Demand Recovery

As can be seen from these management forecasts, no apparent acceleration of revenue is expected by the end of Q2; rather, a repeat of the results of Q1 - low single-digit revenue growth—is expected, and Biotechnology will remain the main driver, while Diagnostics will show the weakest result. During the year, the revenue forecast was not changed, and the EPS range was increased only at the upper limit. Guidance creates a sense of uncertainty, and it does not take into account the Masimo deal.

For comparison, Wall Street expects revenue to grow by 2.60% yoy to about $6.09 billion by the end of the quarter, while adjusted EPS will grow by 2.18% yoy to $1.84. By the end of the year, the consensus forecast for revenue is $25.50 billion (+3.79%), and for EPS $8.44 (+8.17%). Market expectations coincide with management forecasts.

Hold Rating on Moderate Upside and M&A Uncertainty

Let's move on to the main thing, namely the assessment. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2026-2030, discounting the forecast FCFS, and then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030), and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap). With EV/EBITDA, this is the enterprise value from which net debt is also subtracted, which I divide by the number of shares, and get the projected fair value of one share.

At the moment, it is difficult to accurately predict the impact of Masimo on Danaher's final revenue, so I added $50 million to revenue and $125 million in cost savings, as expected by management. As a result, the revenue CAGR of 2026-2030 in my model will be about 5.9%. This is a balanced growth, taking into account M&A transactions, the slower recovery of Life Sciences and the decline of Chinese business due to the intensification of the geopolitical struggle between the United States and China. At the same time, Danaher will invest in increasing profitability and marginality, often even sacrificing business growth rates, but breakthroughs in marginality will also not be achieved due to high competition. Thus, the EBIT margin will grow smoothly from 21.59% in 2024 to 25.67% in 2030. CapEx will remain in the range of 5-5.5% of revenue, which is the historical average for the company. Thus, the average FCF Yield in 2026-2030 will be about 24.5%.

I discounted the forecast FCF for the WACC at 7.67% (all prerequisites are shown in the table above).

The company continues to trade at multiples premiums to the sector median, but premiums have decreased due to the falling share price. Discounts to their historical averages have also increased. Danaher has been trading at a premium for a long time due to the high marginality of the business and good growth rates. But now the growth rate has slowed down, and the uncertainty associated with a major new deal worries investors. Therefore, premiums are reduced.

My DCF model gives the following results: in the baseline scenario, both methods show stock growth of 4-18% within a year. In the most positive scenario, shares should be worth 13-15% more than their current value, and in the most negative scenario, they should be in the range of -3.4-+2.1 %. But despite these results, for now I prefer to just hold the shares I have already bought and wait for news about the specific terms of the deal. Q2 is unlikely to be strong; most likely we will see a weak revenue result again, and this may negatively affect the stock. I prefer to wait it out. Therefore, I am temporarily downgrading the company's rating from Buy to Hold. With strong price drops of 10% or more, it is already possible to look closely at the purchase.

Low Debt Levels with Limited Capital Returns Ahead

Danaher's debt burden remains low in Q1, but it is clear that it will grow significantly by the end of the year. The total debt of the company was about $18.48 billion, there were about $5.70 billion in accounts, and the net debt was $12.78 billion. Thus, the Debt/EBITDA ratio was 2.61x, and the Net Debt/EBITDA was 1.81x. Operating profit exceeds interest expenses by 18 times. In Q1, the company raised its dividend to $0.40 per share with a yield of 0.74% and a payout ratio of about 17%. There were no buybacks in the first quarter, and most likely there will be few in 2026.

Risks

The main risk is the stagnation of demand in the segments of Life Sciences and Diagnostics. There is a feeling that demand will grow more slowly than we would like. Another risk is associated with a potential deal to buy Masimo. This transaction will lead to a strong increase in the debt burden, and the synergistic effects are not guaranteed. The integration may go worse than expected. There is also a geopolitical risk associated with the fact that the company will gradually lose its position in China. The remaining risks are standard-high competition, lag in technological development, problems with the integration of purchased companies, macroeconomic stagnation, and so on.

Final Thoughts

I prefer to wait it out a bit and buy back shares on stronger drawdowns. The potential deal has added a lot of uncertainty, and the revenue growth rate does not meet investors' expectations. Therefore, I am temporarily downgrading the company's rating from Buy to Hold. With strong price drops of 10% or more, it is already possible to look closely at the purchase.

data.title: Yesway: No Way I'm Buying This
data.description:
Introduction

Yesway, Inc. (YSWY) just went public on April 22nd and ended its first day successfully, with 6% up on its $20 initial price offer. I wanted to go through what the company does, its financials, and what is still in store for 2026.

The company

Yesway is a convenience store and fuel retail operator founded in 2015 by Tom Trkla, who is still at the helm of the company. It is built on a classic c-store model, meaning sell fuel to drive traffic and then monetize that traffic through high-margin in-store products, foodservice, and other ancillary services.

Currently, the company operates under two brands, Yesway, which is its flagship brand, primarily located in Midwestern rural and suburban markets, and Allsup’s, which the company acquired back in November 2019 for $850m, expanding its reach by over 300 stores in the Southwest, primarily New Mexico, Texas, and Oklahoma, with a cult following of sorts for its famous deep-fried burrito and chimichangas.

As of the end of 2025, the company had around 419 stores across nine states in the Midwest and Southwest, but it is worth noting that the store count has barely moved since around 2020, when the company hit 402. The company’s growth stalled ever since the attempted 2021-2022 IPO.

The financials

It is not easy to talk about the company’s financials because it just IPO’ed, so there is very little public information available regarding the company. However, we can see that there is quite a decent amount of information already aggregated by many sites, just like Seeking Alpha, which allows us to have a look at the company’s latest financial performance.

Below, I compiled the performance over the last 4 years to see how the company’s operations have developed.

Here, we can see that the company’s revenue growth has been quite unimpressive over the last four years. The biggest standout I can see is that the company’s gross margin expansion was a lot more impressive than its top-line performance. It improved around 340bps from FY22. This tells us that the company likely began to see better fuel procurement terms as its scale grew, as well as a merchandise/foodservice mix shift. Operating margins have somewhat improved as well, but its EBITDA margin seems to be quite sticky at around 5.7% for years, which finally broke out to the top and ended the year 110bps higher than the previous year. Net income has been quite volatile, and it is never really a good indicator of what the company makes because a lot of the time, net income can be affected by one-off charges or increases, which have nothing to do with operations.

What we can also gather from the above table is that YSWY operates with very tight margins, which could be just the nature of the business. To know that, we need to see how its competitors are doing. Below, I compiled the same information, but with some of the competitors to compare.

So, we can see that thin margins are part of the industry. Every single company operates with net margins between 2%-4% and EBITDA margins between 5%-8%. The reason is that most of the revenue runs through the pump at significantly thinner margins. What is important to note is that Yesway’s gross margins are at the top of the range and well above the average because Murphy’s and Sunoco are operating at quite a low margin. So, this is just how the c-store economics work. Don’t expect high margins here.
