Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. According to Seeking Alpha data, Yesway finished the year with around $36.6m in cash and equivalents and $651m in long-term debt. This puts the company’s net debt/EBITDA at around 3.5x. The company raised $280m with the offering as well, so that has improved its position, as the company said most of the raised cash will go towards the reduction of debt.

So, the debt will be reduced by quite a bit, and even before that, the company’s interest coverage ratio at the end of the year was around 2.11x, which, according to many analysts, is healthy. For me, it is a little stretched because it doesn’t give a lot of room for further downside in operations. I’d like to see it closer to 5x so that even if there is a further downturn in operations, the company will still be able to pay its creditors on time and not be at risk of default.

Comments on the Outlook

Since the beginning of the Iran conflict a while back, oil prices saw their craziest volatility in a while. Gas prices are still up considerably from where they were before, and I think this is just going to be a positive for companies like Yesway because, in the short-term, people still need to get to where they need to go, and that means buying gas that is considerably more expensive. Casey’s (CASY) CEO, Darren Rebelez, was recently asked about whether he was seeing any demand destruction because of the gas prices. He said:

We've seen no signs at this stage of the cycle in terms of any change in guest behavior. Certainly, people don't like seeing gas prices go up. Right now, after this last week or so's events, retail's up, give or take, about $0.30 a gallon. At that $0.30 a gallon, now we're in the low $3-a-gallon range on average. That's still 30 cents below the starting point when the Ukraine war began. We typically don't see demand destruction until the retail price of fuel hits closer to $5 per gallon.

That was back in March. Since then, gas prices have stood at around $4 per gallon, meaningfully higher than back in March; however, any meaningful demand destruction only comes at around $5 per gallon, so that means further increases in gas prices will most likely be a tailwind for these gas providers.

Only in the long run will they see a shift in demand, because consumers will have time to adjust their commuting timetable, work more from home, and avoid commuting as much as possible when gas prices are so high. If prices remain at around $4 per gallon, I am expecting a decent first quarter as a public company from Yesway.

Should you buy Yesway?

I would like to see some more numbers regarding the performance, especially up-to-date performance, and I want to hear some comments on what the management thinks is going to happen in 2026 before I consider buying. After a company's IPO, they tend to underperform in the long term after the initial first-day surge, so I don’t see a reason to start right now. I’d like to see the excitement settle down first and see where the company goes over the next few quarters.

data.title: CSX Corporation: The Setup For A Multi-Year Earnings Expansion Story
data.description:
Railroads don’t usually grab headlines. They’re capital intensive, operationally complex, and deeply tied to the broader economy. But every so often, one of them enters a phase where execution, industry dynamics, and internal discipline line up at the same time. That’s where CSX Corporation (CSX) finds itself today.

What looks like a steady, low-growth business on the surface is beginning to show the early signs of a multi-year transformation. The latest results suggest this isn’t just cyclical recovery. It’s structural improvement. Let’s get started on what makes CSX a ‘Buy’ after its recent quarterly earnings!

The Business: A Network That Moves the Economy

CSX operates one of the largest freight rail networks in the eastern U.S. It transports a wide range of goods, including chemicals, agricultural products, metals, coal, and intermodal containers. CSX’s network connects key population centers, ports, and industrial hubs, making it a critical link in domestic and global supply chains.

What makes railroads like CSX compelling is their embedded advantage. Once the infrastructure is in place, incremental volume carries high margins. The challenge has always been execution. Running a rail network efficiently requires precision across scheduling, asset utilization, labor, and capital deployment.

That’s exactly where CSX has been focusing. Recent developments point to a company tightening its operations while simultaneously expanding its opportunity set.

Management is pushing on multiple fronts. This includes improving service reliability, expanding intermodal offerings, and converting freight from trucks to rail. At the same time, major infrastructure projects like the Howard Street Tunnel clearance are set to unlock new capacity and faster transit times across key corridors.

Earnings: Strong Results Driven by Efficiency, Not Just Volume

The most recent quarter marked a clear inflection point. Volume rose 3% and revenue increased 2%, but the real story was below the surface.

Operating expenses fell 6%, leading to a 20% increase in operating income and a 26% jump in EPS. That kind of margin expansion doesn’t happen by accident. It reflects a coordinated push across the organization to eliminate inefficiencies and improve asset utilization.

Management highlighted over $100 million in year-over-year efficiency savings. This was driven by initiatives such as reducing headcount and overtime, shrinking the vehicle fleet and improving fuel efficiency. CSX also delivered record fuel efficiency metrics, underscoring operational improvements at the network level.

Importantly, these gains were broad-based rather than one-off. Cost control has been pushed “to the front lines of the organization,” with accountability extending beyond traditional budget silos. As shown below, CSX improved on all operating metrics of velocity, fuel efficiency, and dwell (idle) time.

On the revenue side, performance was mixed but constructive. Intermodal stood out with 6% volume growth, benefiting from new business wins and improving service. Chemicals also showed strength, supported by demand tied to natural gas and data center-driven energy needs.

This was partly offset by recent headwinds like housing-related forest products and automotive production. Overall, the clear takeaway is that CSX is not relying on a strong macro backdrop. It’s driving earnings growth through internal execution.

Forward Outlook: Structural Tailwinds Begin to Align

Looking ahead, CSX’s outlook combines internal momentum with improving industry dynamics. Management now expects mid-single-digit revenue growth for the full year, along with operating margin expansion toward the high end of a 200–300 basis point range. Free cash flow is projected to grow more than 60% year over year.

That’s a powerful combination, especially for a business often viewed as mature. Several key drivers stand out. First, the shift from truck to rail is gaining traction. Rising diesel prices and tighter trucking capacity are making rail more competitive.

Second, infrastructure investments are unlocking new growth. The Howard Street Tunnel project alone will double capacity on certain routes and reduce transit times by a full day, opening new lanes and improving network efficiency.

Third, the industrial development pipeline provides a steady stream of future volume. These projects, often years in the making, create a built-in growth engine that is less dependent on short-term economic swings. CSX is effectively seeding future volume growth years in advance. This is reflected by roughly 600 active projects and around 100 expected to come online this year.

External trends also support the story. Increasing demand for energy, data center expansion, and ongoing infrastructure spending all play to CSX’s strengths. Even in areas like chemicals and plastics, global supply disruptions are creating opportunities for domestic producers and rail transport by extension.

Financial Position, Dividends, Valuation

One of the more compelling aspects of CSX’s story is its disciplined approach to capital. Management expects capital expenditures to remain below $2.4 billion this year, while still funding key growth and efficiency projects. At the same time, rising free cash flow provides flexibility for shareholder returns.

Management is taking a more analytical approach to capital allocation, emphasizing project-level accountability and data-driven decision-making. This shift matters. Railroads historically struggled with overinvestment and uneven returns. CSX’s focus on productivity, predictive analytics, and targeted spending suggests a more disciplined model going forward.

CSX maintains a BBB+ rated balance sheet with a reasonably safe net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio of 2.9x. While it yields just 1.3%, the dividend is well protected by a 32% payout ratio. It comes with 21 consecutive years of growth and has a 5-year CAGR of 8.5%.

CSX doesn’t look cheap on the surface at the current price of $43.18 and a forward P/E of 23.5. However, the clear efficiency improvements during Q1 and its number of projects suggest a multi-year growth tailwind for the company. CSX is set up for potential EPS growth in the low to mid-teens, which I believe more than justifies the current valuation.

Risks: Macro Sensitivity and Execution Still Matter

The most obvious is macro exposure. CSX’s business is tied to industrial production, housing, and consumer demand. Continued weakness in these areas could limit volume growth, particularly in segments like forest products and automotive.

Fuel prices are another double-edged sword. While higher diesel prices support rail’s competitiveness, they also increase operating costs and can pressure margins.

There are also execution risks. The company is in the early stages of a broad efficiency transformation, with over 100 initiatives underway. Sustaining momentum and embedding these changes into the organization will be critical.

Investor Takeaway: Early in a Multi-Year Re-Rating Story

CSX is not a high-growth tech company, and it doesn’t need to be. What makes it interesting today is the combination of improving execution, structural cost reduction, and emerging growth tailwinds. The latest quarter shows that margin expansion is real, repeatable, and driven by internal actions rather than external luck.

At the same time, intermodal growth, infrastructure upgrades, and industrial development projects are setting the stage for steady volume gains.

The story here is less about rapid expansion and more about consistent compounding. If CSX continues to execute, the market may begin to re-rate the stock from a cyclical operator to a disciplined, efficiency-driven compounder.

That shift doesn’t happen overnight. But the first signs are already visible. Given that CSX is in the early innings of its transition, I rate it as a ‘Buy’ at the current price and valuation.

data.title: CSX: Stellar Financial Report, But Price Risk Still Remains
data.description:
CSX (CSX) reported this afternoon results that match closely on-going railroad car loading data. Going forward, the company continues to invest in long-term growth projects, which involve capacity improvements for containers, returning to service sections of trackage lost in the North Carolina floods and eliminating transportation congestion in locations such as Chicago. America's railroads stand to benefit from long-term changes in the marketplace as well as short-term issues involving energy costs. The stock price of CSX has followed much of the short-term positive changes in the markets. The question becomes how much of the short-term macro-economic translate toward permanent business?

In the railroad investment game of today, critical monitors for investors are simply answering these questions. How location able is the company at inducing modular business? How aligned is the trackage to new manufacturing locations? How willing is management to make long-term investments foregoing short-term returns?

This continues our coverage of CSX, our last being CSX Corporation: Benefiting From A Merger Environment, an article exploring the company's possible merger suitors. Unrealistic as it still seems, strong house cleaning was needed before any merger might be considered. It appears that at least the first needed action began under the new CEO. Still, understanding a more important investment indicator, defining the differences between short-term vs. long-term opportunities and their possible size, remains to be uncovered. Let's go do some searching.

The Railroad

But first let's review the company's physical locations and background. CSX is a railroad formed from a history of storied former flags. It consists of former railroads including B&O, C&O, Western Maryland, Seaboard Coast Line, and major portions of the New York Central. Recently, the Board of Directors stirred up its management team exiting Joseph R. Hinrichs, a former Ford (F) executive, replacing him with Steve Angel. The change seemed targeted at hiring someone more willing to merge with another major railroad such as BNSF, something Hinrichs seemed resistant against.

The Quarter

Now, let's investigate the quarter financial result and management comments. From the presentation, the next slide includes a clear 1st quarter summary.

Notice the significant improvement in operating cash flow and earnings year over year. Management highlighted this change throughout the conference call.

In bullet format, other important metrics follow:

  • 560 bps improvement in margin.
    • For the year, the operating margin is expected to improve by 200-300 basis points. Higher fuel costs will be significant headwinds for an unknown period.
  • Lower operating expenses of approximately $150 million through cost controls (Belt tightening).
    • Train speed improved while diesel efficiency hit 0.97 gallons per 1000 gross ton miles.
  • Intermodular up 6%; coal minus a modest 1%; merchandise unchanged.
    • Intermodular is also being strengthened through improvements from faster service options.
    • Howard Street project is expected to become completely operational in this quarter.
      • Management expects two full bid cycles before the new capacity will be filled.
    • Coal was strong in the 1st quarter of 2025 before falling out of bed during the rest of the year. An even year over year comparison represents a strong 1st quarter.
    • Strong southern deliveries mix.
  • Volumes and revenues improved year over year by 2% and 3% respectively.
  • Strong opportunities in plastics continue driven by global supply imbalances.
  • Housing and auto remain challenging.
  • From the prepared remarks about manufacturing, "Our pipeline of approximately 600 active projects remain strong."
    • 21 projects went into service over the first quarter alone, which should contribute an estimated 33,000 annual carloads at full ramp.

The quarterly report showed a modest gain in the top line with significant improvements in the bottom line through tight management of resources.

Marketplace

Railroads, a necessary component of the American economy, sit within a marketplace generally slowly growing except for energy costs and other issues shifting business from trucks back to rail. For decades, railroads bleed revenue toward the point-to-point faster trucking approach. That exchange is reverting slowly back to the railroad. Under current markets, the higher cost of crude stresses trucking pushing growth into what rail infrastructure can handle. High crude costs have not only increased crude costs but also impacted refining capacity. For example, China purposely shuttered a significant volume of crude selling it to other Asian nations. Tsvetana Paraskova noted on Oilprice, "Sinopec, the world’s biggest refiner by capacity, and Sinochem Group have already sold crude grades from Nigeria and Ghana, mostly to refiners in Asia, including Indonesia and Taiwan, . , ." When world refining shutters, crack spreads increase generally globally. Since the beginning of the conflict with Iran, crack spreads doubled in the U. S. increasing from the lower $20s to the middle $40s. Once again, each of these cost factors influence shifts toward container rail transportation for at least the balance of the year, if not permanently.

A review of more recent carloading data contained in graphical format follows. We included both total car loading and a graph of coal. With higher costs of oil and natural gas prices generally trending higher, coal usage remains strong.

Railcar loadings have trended modestly higher year-over-year.

Again, coal, having been weaker last year, has brightened during 2026. Within its last annual report, management noted,

The Company transports domestic coal, coke and iron ore to electricity-generating power plants, steel manufacturers and industrial plants as well as export coal to deep-water port facilities. Most of the export coal the Company transports is used for steelmaking, while the majority of domestic coal the Company ships is used for electricity generation.

The price of coal remains strong boding well for continued strength which showed an even performance from the strongest performing quarter last year. Remember, the coal business created a significant headwind. So far, not so this year.

Overall, the top line performance matched the weekly data available on the internet.

Investments

The Company invested heavily with government help in several long-range projects including, removing Howard Street tunnel height restrictions and other impediments for hauling double stacks from Philadelphia to Baltimore, rebuilding the Blue Ridge Subdivision, and eliminating the Forest Hill (75th Street) diamonds in lower Chicago. This last improvement is part of a major capacity effort in Chicago to eliminate congestion.

The Howard Street project provides a faster service along the I-95 corridor affecting the Florida to New Jersey service. Noted above, this project will take a few bidding cycles to reach capacity.

Another capital project worth noting includes:

  • "Meridian & Bigbee Railroad, and we will soon be launching improved service with CPKC on our SMX product. SMX provides truck competitive transit between major markets in the southeast with Dallas and Mexico, and recent investments will enhance both speed and efficiency."

A Chart

In essences, similar to the market in general, CSX is extremely overbought shown in the next graphic generated using Power E*TRADE.

Afterhours trading surprised us with its 5% gain. We didn't see anything in this report that justified the major positive reaction. Apparently, we were incorrect; the market did.

Risks

In our view, price risk from short-term related strength might be the biggest risk especially with the large positive reaction in afterhours. We don't believe that oil will remain at elevated levels for very long, thus what is a short-term driver, high energy costs, could become a significant headwind down the road. But, in the long-run, CSX should benefit from trends that will lift revenue.

Summary

Nothing in the CSX report surprised us. It showed modest and predictable top-line growth. Management did claim that efficiency gains reported in the 1st were just a beginning. We aren't sure of the source for future improvements. The charts are super over-bought making it difficult for us to recommend buying stocks in general. Our rating is held at a hold due to valuation. Yet, the company is focusing on the right vectors, long-term manufacturing growth plus inducing intermodular units from trucking. At a lower price in the $30s, CSX will be a buy once again. We do have questions about the size of any future growth opportunities. Most of the opportunities seem targeted at the I-95 corridor, a very small portion of total U. S. markets.
